text: The laparoscopic view reveals bilateral femoral hernias, with the right side appearing larger than the left. The surgeon prepares to dock the XI robot using three ports positioned in the upper abdomen.
The surgeon holds a fenestrated bipolar instrument in the left hand and a scissor in the right hand. A piece of fat is pressed upwards, revealing a clear view into the pelvis without obstruction.

The surgeon begins the procedure on the left side by creating a standard peritoneal flap. The incision starts vertically and somewhat obliquely, then continues transversely and horizontally across the peritoneum.
The surgeon performs a dissection in the preperitoneal plane, avoiding entry into the retrorectus plane, using standard techniques visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon navigates to the space of Retzius, attempting to keep the retrorectus fat elevated while approaching the Cooper's ligament.

The surgeon cuts the medial umbilical ligament using scissors in the right hand, which was previously inhibiting movement. In the lateral space of Bogros, the surgeon carefully hugs the peritoneum during dissection.
The surgeon carefully advances towards the deep pelvis, using retraction to expose and manipulate the cotton candy-like areolar tissue, expanding the surgical space.

The surgeon focuses on achieving hemostasis while dissecting deep into the pelvis near the femoral vessels, obturator structures, and vas deferens, ensuring careful separation to avoid injury to critical anatomical structures.

The surgeon is working deep into the pelvis, focusing on taking down structures in the upper pelvis to facilitate deeper dissection. The anatomy appears normal, which is atypical in patients with inguinal hernias.

The surgeon navigates deeper into the herniated femoral contents, encountering a large piece of fat protruding into the femoral space. The preperitoneal fat appears to be herniating, and the large femoral vein or external iliac vein is visible as it transitions into the femoral vein.
The surgeon carefully identifies the natural plane between the bladder and lateral structures, using tension and counter tension to reveal the edge of the peritoneum.
The surgeon creates lateral space for mesh placement, revealing the edge of the peritoneum. A small hole in the peritoneum is visible, which will be repaired later.

The surgeon employs a no-touch technique to avoid contact with the vas deferens and testicular vessels, minimizing the risk of nerve injury. The mesh is positioned deep within the space of Retzius. The obturator nerve is visible, with the surgeon carefully dissecting below it using minimal cautery. The obturator artery comes into view, and the dissection is clear and straightforward due to the absence of blood staining the tissue.

The surgeon carefully peels back tissue along the peritoneum, aiming to position the mesh deeply within the pelvis. The dissection involves skeletonizing the vas deferens to ensure the mesh transitions smoothly from the deep pelvis to the lateral pelvis, avoiding interference from the bladder or peritoneum.

The surgeon divides the medial umbilical ligament to clear the surgical field. Fat over Cooper's ligament is being removed to expose the ligament for suturing, aiming to place stitches close to the vas deferens to secure the mesh placement.

The surgeon decides to leave the fat intact on the opposite side, opting to palpate and visualize Cooper's ligament through the fat without dividing it. On the right side, nodal structures were divided, leading to lymphatic leakage, which was managed by placing clips on the affected nodes.

The surgeon navigates medially and posteriorly, identifying the medial umbilical ligament, which is not the ureter as it passes medial to the vas deferens. Despite the ligament not obstructing the surgical field, it is divided to ensure the mesh placement is low for addressing a bilateral femoral hernia. Clips are applied to the ligament to manage potential bleeding from the vessel within.

The surgeon inspects the left side for a corolipoma but decides not to remove the small amount of fat present. The dissection on the left side is complete, and the focus shifts to the larger right side for further surgical intervention.

The surgeon begins creating a flap near the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS), making an oblique vertical incision and extending it across. The flap development on this side appears more challenging, possibly due to a higher position, prompting a cautious approach to avoid tearing the perineum. The flap on this side visually differs from the flap on the opposite side.

The surgeon transitions from the visceral space against the peritoneum to the parietal space against the rectus muscle, dividing the attenuated posterior rectus sheath. Two distinct layers of the posterior rectus sheath are visible above the arcuate line, along with the inferior epigastric vessels. Retrorectus fat is left intact if not too thick, as the surgeon joins the dissection completed on the left side.

The surgeon identifies a small indirect inguinal hernia and an additional interparietal hernia, reducing incarcerated fat from the hernia site. The surgeon plans to cover the area with mesh used for the primary hernia repair. The surgical field shows lateral positioning, prompting the surgeon to adjust the dissection to stay between the peritoneum and extraperitoneal fatty tissue.

The surgeon re-enters the plane adjacent to the peritoneum, identifying the vas deferens and a small amount of fat herniating lateral to the inferior epigastric vessels but medial to the internal ring. The surgeon begins dissecting the contents of the femoral hernia, noting the complexity of the work in this area. Some herniated fat is removed while ensuring no injury to large branches of the gastric vessels.

The surgeon achieves hemostasis, allowing for smooth and quick progression through the tissue layers. The femoral hernia sac is visible and is being extracted clearly, providing a comprehensive view of the surgical field.

The surgeon encounters the medial umbilical ligament obstructing the dissection and divides both medial umbilical ligaments. The dissection is complicated by scarring, with the medial umbilical ligament and peritoneum involved in the femoral hernia. Despite the challenges, the contents are successfully reduced, revealing the tip of the contents, with minimal tissue left inside the femoral canal.

The surgeon identifies and begins dissecting a cord lipoma, carefully teasing it out from surrounding fatty tissue. The natural capsule of the lipoma is separated from the testicular vessels. The vas deferens is visible, and the surgeon proceeds cautiously to distinguish between the testicular vessels and the cord lipoma.
The surgeon carefully separates the fatty cord tissue from the sacrum vessels, ensuring that the testicular vessels remain undamaged.

The surgeon navigates around retroperitoneal vascular attachments while handling cord lipomas, avoiding posterior dissection to prevent damage to lymphatics and the genital femoral nerve. A larger piece of retroperitoneal fat is left attached, and a mesh is placed anteriorly.

The surgeon disconnects tethered fat to lay mesh on the cord structures, continuing dissection to separate a large tongue of peritoneum from a sling. Good retraction reveals the plane, showing nodal tissue on the left side, which is left in place. The longest obturator artery is visible, extending deep into the space of Retzius.

The surgeon navigates deep into the obturator space, aiming to place the mesh approximately three centimeters deep. The lymphatic tissue is left intact, and a natural tissue plane is identified between the bladder and lymphatic tissue. The obturator fat is typically left in place, but the space is prepared for potential deep mesh placement.

The surgeon identifies the obturator internus muscle and skeletonizes the vas deferens. The peritoneum is peeled back to prevent it from slipping behind the mesh. The obturator nerve is visible, along with the femoral branch and genitofemoral nerve, as the surgeon creates space for the mesh in the femoral space. An interparietal hernia is observed.

The surgeon selects a flat sheet pro grip mesh, measuring 20 by 15, and trims it to fit the anatomical space required for the interparietal hernia repair. A tape measure is used to assess the dimensions of the surgical site to ensure the mesh is appropriately sized.

The surgeon measures the space for mesh placement, noting the mesh should lay at an angle rather than directly transversely. A trimmed flat sheet pro grip mesh is brought in, but it appears too small for the intended area.

The surgeon removes the initial piece of mesh, using it as a stencil to cut a new, appropriately sized mesh piece. The large surgical space is visible, indicating the need for significant mesh overlap. The first piece of mesh appears small for the space, but the surgeon places it to assess its fit.

The surgeon places the ProGrip mesh obliquely over the surgical site, attempting to achieve good posterior and deep femoral coverage. However, the mesh is visibly too short for immediate coverage, highlighting its insufficient width for the space.

The surgeon uses a 20 by 15 centimeter piece of mesh, trimming the corners for better posterior placement. The mesh is measured and adjusted to ensure adequate coverage, particularly at the transition from the Bagros space to the space of Retzius, to prevent peritoneal intrusion. The surgeon rolls the mesh back up for removal before introducing the newly adjusted mesh.
The surgeon measures the surgical space more diagonally, including the femoral space, to ensure accurate placement of the mesh.

The surgeon exchanges the previous mesh for a newer, wider piece. The mesh is partially unfurled to facilitate easier placement. The ProGrip mesh adheres to the tissue, providing confidence that the peritoneum will not intrude behind it.

The surgeon places the mesh deep into the metropubic space, approximately three centimeters deep, ensuring the femoral space remains covered. The mesh is unfolded across the area, covering an unusual interparietal hernia without sutures due to its small size. Cooper's ligament is visible through the mesh and fat, providing a secure anchoring point. The mesh is adjusted for more overlap near the lateral femoral space.

The surgeon observes a portion of the mesh folded over the femoral space, then uses a 3-0 polysorb suture to secure the mesh to Cooper's ligament at two points due to a wide direct defect. The mesh is visible through the fat, with a vessel located near the stitch placement. The mesh adheres well in most areas, including posterior, lateral, superior, and medial regions, ensuring coverage and preventing the bladder and peritoneum from protruding.

The surgeon identifies the obturator nerve and ensures the mesh is in contact without causing future issues. The mesh provides good coverage of the interparietal hernia. The surgeon prepares to place the left-sided mesh, observing the seam of the anatomical mesh and positioning it for adequate coverage.

The surgeon observes clear fluid trickling from the divided fatty tissue and places clips on both the proximal and distal sides to manage the drainage. The leakage becomes more apparent after the clips are applied.

The surgeon identifies a site of tissue leakage and places an additional clip to address the issue. Following this, the surgeon proceeds to unfurl the remaining portion of the mesh over the surgical area.

The surgeon unfolds the mesh, ensuring the lower flap is completely free and placed down for good coverage. The mesh is positioned deep with posterior overlap, and two stitches are placed in Cooper's ligament, one lateral and one medial, due to the large femoral space. Vessels are visible through the mesh, and the peritoneal flaps are closed to complete the operation.
